summary: K=10 / degree=4 / 20k shots gives a high-precision estimate of the QPU noise floor: P(AUX=1) = 0.2864 with a 95 percent CI of [0.2801, 0.2927].  Classical tail = 0.0095, so the rel error is 29x — almost 3x worse than the noisy 256-shot 12x estimate.  The 256-shot run was a fluke (1.5 sigma below the true value).  Even at 20k shots, mitigation has near-zero effect (Heron readout is 0.2-0.5 percent), confirming that gate decoherence is the dominant error source at this depth.
takeaways: The 20k shot run shows the QPU noise floor for K=10, degree 4, depth 919, is P(AUX=1) = 0.2864 with 0.6 percent CI.  This is the 'true' QPU behaviour; the 256-shot 0.12 was a 1.5-sigma fluctuation below it.
Classical tail = 0.0095 is 30x below the QPU 95 percent CI lower bound (0.28).  Rel error 29x is the honest number; the 12x reported earlier was overoptimistic.
Readout mitigation is a no-op at this scale (e10 = 0.2 percent, e01 = 0.5 percent, condition number 1.007).  The bottleneck is gate decoherence on the 919-depth Heron circuit, not measurement error.

K=10 still gives a 28 percent Heron-depth reduction vs K=17 (919 vs 1285), the compiled-circuit topology is fundamentally cheaper.  The 12x-vs-29x gap is the difference between a low-stat fluke and the true noise floor.

Next steps to close the gap: (a) Tier 2 step 7 — QROM-based amplitude loading; (b) reduce degree at K=10 to degree 2; (c) ZNE folding.  All target the gate-decoherence floor, which is now the limiting factor.
